paper_title: Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Languages with rich static semantics, such as Rust, provide stronger guarantees for AI-generated code, but their strictness makes generation more difficult. Off-the-shelf compilers can provide useful feedback post-generation, but does not guide intermediate generation steps, such as those during autoregressive LLM decoding. Constrained decoding intervenes earlier by rejecting invalid tokens during sampling, but requires white-box model access and costly reimplementation for semantic constraints.We introduce generative compilation, the first approach to obtaining compiler feedback on partial programs during generation. The core technical device is a sealor: a lightweight, mostly syntax-guided transformation that converts partial programs into complete ones that standard compilers can diagnose. It is designed such that possible-to-complete partial programs are never rejected, while preserving enough code context to catch genuine dead ends early. We construct such a sealor on a core Rust-like calculus and prove that it satisfies these properties, all mechanized in Lean. We extend it to the first partial-program checker for real Rust. We evaluate our method on challenging repository-level Rust coding tasks, across both frontier black-box and open-weight models. We show that generative compilation reduces non-compiling outputs and improves functional correctness, relative to standard post-generation feedback. It does so by detecting a broad range of errors close to their source and early during generation, thereby reducing errors cascades and enabling focused diagnostics. More broadly, generative compilation is a step toward making compilers a first-class citizen of AI-assisted programming active during generation, rather than a separate post-generation check.

sections.title:
1 Introduction
AI has become a standard tool for code generation, driven by the growing capabilities of large
language models (LLMs) and LLM-based coding agents [17]. Yet AI-generated code remains error-
prone, threatening the correctness and security of downstream software systems [42, 54]. This
makes languages with rich static semantics particularly valuable for AI-generated code. Rust, for
example, uses its ownership and borrowing discipline to provide memory safety guarantees and
prevent related reliability and security failures [19, 27]. At the same time, these guarantees are
enforced through strict compile-time rules, making valid code harder to generate than in more
permissive languages such as C [8, 20, 59]. Compiler feedback can help bridge this gap by identifying
violations and guiding the model toward code that satisfies these rules.
sections.content: Post-Generation Compiler Feedback. The most common way to leverage compiler feedback is to wait until entire files are fully generated, run the compiler as is on the resulting program, and feed any diagnostics back to the model. This post-hoc feedback loop has important practical advantages: it works with black-box LLMs and benefits from the compilers guarantees and diagnostics with no Both authors contributed equally to this research. We publicly release our proof mechanization, code implementation, benchmarks, and evaluation results: https://github. com/eth-sri/generative-compilation. Authors Contact Information: Niels Mündler-Sasahara, ETH Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland, niels.muendler@inf.ethz.ch; Hristo Venev, INSAIT and Sofia University St. Kliment Ohridski, Sofia, Bulgaria, hristo.venev@insait.ai; Dawn Song, University of California, Berkeley, Berkeley, CA, USA, dawnsong@berkeley.edu; Martin Vechev, ETH Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland, martin.vechev@inf.ethz.ch; Jingxuan He, University of California, Berkeley, Berkeley, CA, USA, jingxuan.he@berkeley.edu. 1:2 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He additional implementation cost [5, 9, 56]. However, it does not take advantage of the intermediate outputs produced during generation. Feedback becomes available only after the complete program has been produced, meaning that every token emitted after the first unrecoverable error may be based on invalid assumptions and ultimately wasted. Moreover, since the feedback concerns the entire output, the model receives batch of accumulated diagnostics all at once, which can be large enough to hinder model understanding [10, 24]. Constrained Decoding. Constrained decoding instead intervenes directly during generation. It checks intermediate outputs of LLMs, accepting only tokens that keep the current output extendable to valid program [23, 31, 39, 43, 53]. Thus, if generation terminates, the final output is guaranteed to be valid. However, for languages with expressive static semantics, achieving this guarantee requires substantial language-specific reimplementation, making it effectively impractical to support more than restricted fragments [29, 31, 34]. Moreover, token-level filtering is silent: the model is steered away from invalid continuations, but never receives compiler-style explanations that could help it revise the code. Finally, constrained decoding requires white-box access to the models next-token distribution and sampling process, ruling out its application to frontier models exposed only through black-box APIs [33, 53]. This Work: Generative Compilation. We introduce generative compilation, the first approach to make compiler-style feedback available during code generation. It serves as middle ground between post-generation feedback and constrained decoding, addressing key limitations of both. Like constrained decoding, generative compiler processes partial programs while they are being produced. Unlike constrained decoding, however, it provides compiler-style diagnostics upon rejection. The model receives an explanation of what went wrong and can revise the generated code, rather than being silently steered away from invalid tokens. Because the feedback is obtained on partial code, it concerns only isolated coding mistakes and avoids contextual clutter. Further, the technique works with black-box models and reuses existing compiler infrastructure. To enable generative compilation, the core new concept we introduce is sealor: transformation that closes partial program into complete one by filling in missing syntactic structure and inserting well-typed placeholders. The resulting sealed program can then be checked by an existing compiler. The key challenge is designing the sealor to achieve both faithful feedback and error coverage. Compiler rejection of the sealed program should guarantee that the partial program truly requires revision. At the same time, sealing should expose as many dead ends as possible, so that unrecoverable errors are caught early. We formalize these properties through corresponding completeness and soundness guarantees. Our key insight is that lightweight, mostly syntax-guided sealor can already meet the guarantees we target, without reimplementing the target languages type system. We first demonstrate this on Featherweight Rust (FR), compact calculus inspired by Rusts ownership and borrowing discipline [41]. We develop sealor for FR and prove that it satisfies the desired properties, with the full development mechanized in Lean. We then carry the same methodology to real Rust: the sealor preserves generated syntax, inserts small set of placeholders for missing code, and delegates type, borrow, and lifetime reasoning to rustc, the Rust compiler. We perform an extensive evaluation across seven frontier black-box and open-weight LLMs. We focus on two repository-level code generation tasks for Rust, designed to stress compiler feedback: C-to-Rust translation and code generation against recently updated library APIs. The results show that, compared with post-generation feedback, the on-the-fly feedback loop enabled by generative compilation further reduces compiler errors and improves functional correctness in most model-task configurations. We then analyze how: generative compilation provides more focused diagnostics that are close to the error source and available well before file completion. Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:3 In summary, our main contributions are: Key Contributions. We introduce generative compilation, novel framework enabling off-the-shelf compilers to check partial programs during code generation. We define the notion of sealors and prove that their completeness and soundness lift to generative compilation. (3) We instantiate generative compilation on FR, presenting syntax-guided sealor that never rejects partial programs that admit valid completion and correctly flags an important class of partial programs with no valid extension. We fully mechanize FR, our sealor, and its guarantees in Lean, making number of corrections to the original FR formalization in the process. (4 and 5) We apply our methodology to real Rust and build the first partial-program checker for Rust. This is based on practical sealor that delegates semantic checking to rustc while handling Rust-specific syntax, control flow, placeholders, and future-dependent errors. (6) We evaluate our approach extensively on Rust coding tasks across frontier and open-weight models, showing that it reduces compiler errors, improves functional correctness, and detects various errors early during generation. (7)

sections.title:
2 Motivation for Generative Compilation
Today’s AI-based code generation is largely powered by LLMs. However, LLM-generated code
provides no guarantee of respecting the target language’s static rules. Two existing strategies aim
to address this gap: post-generation compiler feedback and constrained decoding. In this section,
we present both strategies and use a running example to show their limitations, thereby motivating
generative compilation.
sections.content: Code Generation with Language Models. Let 𝑀 denote an LLM that generates code autoregressively, one token at time from left to right. These tokens are drawn from fixed vocabulary Σ, determined by 𝑀s tokenizer. Modern LLM tokenizers are universal: any string can be constructed from tokens in Σ [49]. We therefore take Σ to be the domain of all strings. 3 1 fn fmt_msg(msg: &mut String) -> String { 2 let tag = &msg[..HEADER_SIZE]; msg.drain(..HEADER_SIZE); let prio = tag.starts_with("HIGH"); Given prompt 𝑥 (a natural-language task, optionally with reference code) and the code generated so far 𝑐, 𝑀 produces next-token distribution 𝑀 ( 𝑥 𝑐), where denotes string concatenation. token 𝑡 𝑀 ( 𝑥 𝑐) is sampled, appended to 𝑐, and generation repeats until special end-of-sequence token EOS is produced. As discussed above, this process can generate any string in Σ. However, for target language 𝐿 Σ, there is no priori guarantee that the final output lies in 𝐿. For example, when prompted to write Rust function that strips fixed-size header from message and formats the result for logging, an LLM might produce the code in Fig. 1. Running the Rust compiler on this program reveals that it cannot be compiled. Fig. 1. Non-compilable LLM-generated Rust code. format!("{} {}: {}", prio, n, body) let = body.lines().count(); let body = msg; 8 } 6 4 7 Post-Generation Compiler Feedback. Most programming languages provide practical checker that decides, for given string 𝑐, whether it belongs to the language 𝐿, i.e., whether 𝑐 is syntactically well-formed and type checks. Although compilers perform many tasks, such as optimizing code and emitting assembly, it is common to refer to this checking phase as compiling; following this convention, we call the checker compiler in this paper. Formally, we model the compiler as function : Σ Σ, which maps an input string 𝑐 to pair (ok, err) consisting of Boolean verdict and textual diagnostic. If 𝑐 𝐿, then C(𝑐) = (true, ""), i.e., the textual diagnostic is empty; otherwise, C(𝑐) = (false, err), where err "" is an error 1:4 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He error[E0502]: cannot borrow *msg as mutable because it is also borrowed as immutable 2 let tag = &msg[..HEADER_SIZE]; 3 4 --- immutable borrow occurs here msg.drain(..HEADER_SIZE); ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ mutable borrow occurs here let prio = tag.starts_with("HIGH"); --- immutable borrow later used here Fig. 2. Error message returned by the Rust compiler after it rejects the program in Fig. 1. message explaining why 𝑐 𝐿, e.g., by highlighting failed type check, and potentially suggesting how to resolve the issue, e.g., by suggesting type cast. The compiler can be naturally used in feedback loop for LLM-based code generation: after the model generates full program 𝑐, we obtain (ok, err) C(𝑐). If ok = true, generation terminates and 𝑐 is guaranteed to be an element of 𝐿. Otherwise, the model is asked to try again with the original prompt augmented by the generated program and diagnostic, e.g., 𝑥 𝑐 err. For the example program in Fig. 1, running the compiler returns (false, err), with err shown in Figure 2. The error involves Rusts ownership, borrowing, and lifetime discipline: value may be accessed through either shared immutable references or an exclusive mutable reference, but not both during overlapping lifetimes. At Line 2, the slice tag is an immutable borrow of msg. The call to msg.drain at Line 3 then attempts to mutably borrow msg while that immutable borrow is still live, because tag is later used at Line 4. This diagnostic is then appended to the prompt, and the model is asked to fix the error, either by regenerating the program from scratch or by editing the existing program. Fig. 3 shows valid outcome that fixes the error by making tag an owned copy of the header rather than borrow from msg. The temporary immutable borrow used to create this copy ends before msg.drain takes mutable borrow, so the two borrows do not overlap and the program satisfies Rusts rules. 3 1 fn fmt_msg(msg: &mut String) -> String { 2 let tag = msg[..HEADER_SIZE].to_string(); msg.drain(..HEADER_SIZE); let prio = tag.starts_with("HIGH"); This feedback loop pays for its simplicity through delayed feedback. In Fig. 1, the borrow conflict already manifests at Line 4. However, compiler feedback is obtained only after the model finishes generating the entire function at Line 8. For longer programs in practice, early mistakes can influence subsequent generation and snowball before the model receives any signal from the compiler. As result, additional mistakes can be introduced and multiple errors may be reported together in single diagnostic bundle, making each repair attempt harder and slowing the overall trial-anderror design process. Fig. 3. The regenerated Rust program above compiles after post-generation compiler feedback round. format!("{} {}: {}", prio, n, body) let = body.lines().count(); let body = msg; 8 } 5 4 7 Constrained Decoding. Constrained decoding offers an alternative to compiler feedback loops. It exploits the autoregressive nature of LLM generation by intervening during decoding, rather than waiting until the entire program has been emitted. Specifically, constrained decoding relies on prefix checker : Σ B, where (𝑐) = true if the current output 𝑐 can still be extended into full program in 𝐿, or formally, if 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿. At each decoding step, candidate tokens are filtered through P, so that only tokens 𝑡 satisfying (𝑐 𝑡) = true may be selected. As result, invalid Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1: continuations are ruled out as soon as they arise, and every generated partial program remains prefix of some valid program in 𝐿. Prefix checking solves different problem from conventional compilation: the former must reason about partial programs, whereas the latter assumes full programs as input. This creates major implementation burden. Existing compiler infrastructure cannot be used directly, so constrained decoding systems must often reimplement the relevant language semantics for prefix checking. For general-purpose programming languages, matching the behavior of the full compiler in this way is impractical. For example, the compiler-related part of the Rust codebase alone contains more than 600k lines of code [47]. As result, existing constrained decoding systems either enforce only syntax [4, 39, 53] or cover restricted subset of typing [31, 34]. 3 let tag = &msg[..HEADER_SIZE]; msg.drain(..HEADER_SIZE); 1 fn fmt_msg(msg: &mut String) -> String { 2 Assume for now that (well-implemented) constrained decoding engine for Rust is available, and let us again examine the example program in Fig. 1. Constrained decoding has no effect as long as each next token keeps the partial output extendable to valid program. Thus, the model generates the same prefix up to let prio = tag, shown in Fig. 4. This prefix is still valid: tag may refer to the prefix of an identifier to be defined later, such as function name. However, when selecting the next token, the . used in the original program must be rejected by prefix checking. Choosing . commits tag to the existing variable, which triggers the same borrow conflict. Therefore, to remain on valid path, the model must choose the unnatural use-before-definition continuation. Fig. 4. Constrained decoding rejects . as it leads to dead-end program prefix. let prio = tag. 4 This example exposes two further costs of constrained decoding. First, constrained decoding silently filters out the token . and can only shape future continuations. The model is never told why the token was rejected, nor can it revise the already generated prefix, as the repair in Fig. 3 would require. The only way forward is to force the model onto the low-probability use-before-definition path described above. This can degrade global generation quality, especially when the rejection is due to non-local semantic constraints such as Rusts borrow checking [4, 23, 39]. Second, filtering and resampling tokens require white-box access to the models next-token distribution, which rules out the closed-weight frontier models that currently dominate coding workflows in practice. Generative Compilation on the Running Example. Prior work has made valuable progress on addressing the limitations of constrained decoding from the perspective of probabilistic sampling [4, 12, 23, 25, 39, 45, 52]. In contrast, our generative compilation approach aims to explore different route. Like constrained decoding, it operates on partial programs rather than waiting for full program. Like conventional compilation, however, it produces textual feedback rather than altering the sampling process. Crucially, it does so through lightweight implementation that largely reuses existing compiler infrastructure and supports black-box models available only through APIs. As result, generative compilation addresses the limitations of the two strategies above. We now use the running example to sketch how generative compilation handles partial programs. The key idea behind it is to seal partial programs, turning them into full programs that can be checked by conventional compiler. From the discussion above, the error in Fig. 1 already manifests when generation reaches the prefix in Fig. 4, including .. There, the program has committed to referring tag to the previously defined variable, which violates Rusts borrow-checking rules. Through series of rule applications, our sealor transforms this partial program into the full program shown in Fig. 5. The sealed program preserves the access to tag at Line 4. It then inserts generic placeholder, holeval() at Line 5, which represents an arbitrary well-typed value. In this 1:6 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He example, it is value of the functions String return type. Finally, the sealor closes the function at Line 6 such that the program is syntactically well-formed. 3 let tag = &msg[..HEADER_SIZE]; msg.drain(..HEADER_SIZE); 1 fn fmt_msg(msg: &mut String) -> String { Compiling the sealed program exposes the same semantic issue as the full program in Fig. 1: tag still borrows from msg across the call to msg.drain. Thus, the Rust compiler rejects Fig. 5 with similar borrow-checking diagnostic. Compared with the partial program in Fig. 4, the sealed program has only inserted holeval(), which does not introduce type errors, and completed the missing syntax. Thus, the borrow-checking error reported on the sealed program reflects genuine error already committed by the partial program. This is one of the guarantees we aim to obtain from the sealor: compiler errors on sealed programs should correspond to errors in the original partial programs, rather than artifacts of sealing. Fig. 5. full Rust program produced by our sealor. &tag; holeval () 6 } 4 We then map the diagnostic produced on the sealed program back to the partial program by comparing source-code spans. The model receives the partial program together with the mapped diagnostic, but never sees the sealed program itself. With this feedback, it ultimately produces the same repaired program as in Fig. 3, making tag an owned copy rather than borrow from msg. Generative compilation therefore provides the relevant feedback four and half lines earlier than post-generation feedback, when the error first becomes unavoidable.

3.1 Generative Compilers and Sealors
Generative Compilers: Definition. A generative compiler is a function G : Σ∗ → B × Σ∗. That
is, it has the same interface as a conventional compiler and returns a Boolean verdict ok together
with a textual diagnostic err. However, instead of checking whether a complete program belongs to
𝐿, it performs prefix checking, i.e., whether a partial program 𝑐 admits a valid completion. Unlike
constrained decoding, a generative compiler retains compiler diagnostics: err is empty whenever
ok = true and otherwise provides a textual explanation, rather than a silent rejection signal.
sections.content: Generative Compilers: Completeness and Soundness. We say that generative compiler accepts 𝑐 when it returns positive verdict on 𝑐, i.e., when G(𝑐) = (true, ""). It is complete on class of programs Σ if for all 𝑐 X, 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿 implies that accepts 𝑐. It is sound on if for all 𝑐 X, accepts 𝑐 implies 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿. Intuitively, completeness rules out rejecting partial programs that admit valid extensions, whereas soundness rules out accepting dead-end partial programs. We say is globally complete or sound, when = Σ. is exact on iff it is sound and complete on X. Note that the first output of G(𝑐) can be viewed as prefix checker, inheriting the corresponding notions of soundness and completeness [31]. Sealors: Definition. Generative compilation seeks to reuse the existing compiler to obtain rich feedback and avoid costly reimplementation, while checking partial programs during generation. This is achieved through new notion of sealing, which we define now. sealor is lightweight, language-specific transformation : Σ Σ that attempts to close the unfinished structure of partial program. Its goal is to produce closed form that conventional compiler can process, thereby obtaining verdict and diagnostic. Given compiler and sealor S, the induced generative compiler is defined by GC,S (𝑐) C(S(𝑐)). This definition abstracts away practical mismatch: diagnostics produced by refer Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:7 to the sealed program S(𝑐), whereas the model should receive feedback on the original partial program 𝑐. This can be addressed by post-processing diagnostics, for example, by mapping source spans in S(𝑐) back to their corresponding spans in 𝑐. We discuss this implementation detail for Rust in 6.1; for simplicity, we assume for now that diagnostics are reported on the corresponding spans of the partial program 𝑐. Sealors: Completeness and Soundness. We define completeness and soundness for sealors analogously to generative compilers. Definition 3.1 (Sealor Completeness and Soundness). sealor is complete on Σ if, for every 𝑐 X, 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿 implies S(𝑐) 𝐿. It is sound on if, for every 𝑐 X, S(𝑐) 𝐿 implies 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿. It is globally complete (resp. sound) when = Σ. Intuitively, completeness requires that sealing turns every prefix with valid continuation into valid program, whereas soundness requires that sealing not make dead-end prefix appear valid. Assuming is exact, the completeness and soundness of sealor correspond directly to those of its induced generative compiler. Theorem 3.2 (Sealor Completeness and Soundness lift to the Generative Compiler). Let be exact and Σ. If is complete on X, then GC,S is complete on X. Symmetrically, if is sound on X, then GC,S is sound on X. Proof. For completeness: take 𝑐 such that 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿. Completeness of on gives S(𝑐) 𝐿; exactness of then gives that accepts S(𝑐), i.e., that GC,S accepts 𝑐. For soundness: take 𝑐 with GC,S accepting 𝑐, i.e., accepts S(𝑐). Exactness of gives S(𝑐) 𝐿; soundness of on then gives 𝑤 . 𝑐 𝑤 𝐿. These characterizations give semantic proof obligations for sealor, which can be established from the metatheory of the target language and then lifted to completeness or soundness of the induced generative compiler. We dive deep into this for our FR sealor in 5. Target Guarantees: Global Completeness and Selective Soundness. Achieving both global completeness and global soundness amounts to exact prefix checking, which is undecidable in general for expressive semantic constraints [34]. Constrained decoding faces this difficulty at every decoding step. Because emitted tokens cannot be revisited, it typically requires global soundness to inductively ensure that generation always remains on path that admits valid extension. Token-level completeness is not required, but highly desirable: rejecting token prematurely that could still lead to valid program may significantly degrade model performance under constraints [4, 50]. Maintaining both properties token by token pushes constrained decoding toward exact prefix checking. When constrained decoding should support rich static semantics, this causes substantial reimplementation effort of the corresponding semantic analysis such as typing [31]. For generative compilation, the priorities for soundness and completeness change, with incompleteness becoming the more harmful failure mode. Rejecting partial program that admits valid continuation causes the model to discard or revise prefix from which correct program could still be generated, providing spurious feedback that can confuse the model. Meanwhile, soundness can be relaxed. rejection triggers revision: the model regenerates based on diagnostic feedback, allowing to revise already generated tokens. Missing soundness violation is thus tolerable: the generative compiler may accept dead-end prefix and allow generation to continue, but the error can still be caught by later check or by final compilation, when more code context is available. Nevertheless, soundness remains valuable where it can be obtained cheaply, because it lets on-thefly feedback arrive as early as possible. Therefore, for generative compilation, we target global completeness together with soundness on selected class of programs Σ. 1:8 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He LLM-Based Code Generation

We combine generative compilation and LLM-based code generation as two (concurrent) modules. The LLM streams each generated prefix 𝑐 to the generative compiler; upon rejection, the generative compiler returns an augmented prompt containing 𝑐 and its diagnostic err, causing generation to restart. The two modules communicate only through plain text, supporting black-box LLM APIs.

sections.title:
3.2 Combining Generative Compilation and LLM-Based Code Generation
Fig. 6 shows the approach we use in this paper to combine generative compilation with LLM-
based code generation: the two run as (concurrent) modules that exchange only plain text. Other
integration strategies are possible; we discuss them in §8.
sections.content: The module on the left represents LLM-based code generation. It performs standard autoregressive decoding, as discussed in 2. Generation is interrupted and restarted whenever the module receives new prompt 𝑥 from generative compilation. As token generation proceeds, the module sends the latest partial program 𝑐 to generative compilation, stopping when EOS is emitted. This module does not require access to 𝑀s token distribution or control over its sampling procedure. It only observes the current partial program 𝑐 as it is generated. For frontier black-box models, this can be obtained through streaming API responses, where output is returned incrementally rather than only after completion. Although Fig. 6 illustrates communication at token granularity, the same design also works when the API returns larger chunks of text. The module on the right performs generative compilation. It checks received prefixes using latest-wins strategy: incoming prefixes do not interrupt ongoing validation, and prefixes received during validation are coalesced so that only the newest one is retained. When validation of prefix 𝑐 finishes with (ok, err) GC,S (𝑐), the module acts on the verdict. If ok, it augments the prompt with 𝑐 and err and sends the revised prompt to the generator. If ok holds and 𝑐 ends with EOS, it returns 𝑐 as the generated program. Otherwise, 𝑐 is valid partial program, and the module begins validating the latest buffered prefix, if one is available. In the common case where the EOS-terminated prefix is already complete program, the final check coincides with ordinary post-generation compiler feedback. More generally, generative compilation and post-generation feedback are complementary and can always be combined. When configured to run concurrently, generative compilation does not block token generation: the LLM can continue producing code while one prefix is being validated. This avoids waiting for compiler result after every token when validation is slower than token sampling. The latest-wins strategy then retains only the newest prefix generated during validation for the next check. As result, rejection may arrive after the LLM has produced few additional tokens. This extra work is modest and limited to the short suffix generated during single validation, and once the earlier prefix is rejected, its continuation is discarded in the restarted attempt. When incremental compilation is available, as in rustc, compilation speed can increase significantly. Generative compilation benefits also from this, since it is built largely on existing compiler infrastructure. sections.title:
4 FR: A Core Calculus for Rust
In this section, we review Featherweight Rust [41], a compact calculus inspired by Rust’s borrow
system. We present the syntax (§4.1) and typing (§4.2) needed to demonstrate our generative
compilation approach in §5, omitting details that are not essential here. For the full calculus, we
refer readers to Pearce [41].
sections.content: Why Core Calculus. To present and analyze our approach formally, we need language formalization. Since our target is Rust, this means formalization for Rust, and it must satisfy two requirements. First, it should remain close to Rusts surface syntax, because our approach directly manipulates Rust source code. Second, it should provide compact setting in which our approach can be defined, analyzed, and understood, while still capturing Rusts most prominent features. Existing Rust formalizations do not simultaneously meet these requirements. RustBelt [19], RustHorn [28], and Aeneas [15] provide powerful foundations for semantic reasoning and verification, but they operate at level of abstraction that is not sufficiently close to the source syntax, and their developments are necessarily substantial. Oxide [57] is closer to Rusts surface syntax, but still introduces significant machinery, making it more elaborate than needed for our purpose. Why FR. Featherweight Rust (FR) [41] follows the philosophy of Featherweight Java [16]: provide concise and minimal proof of type soundness while preserving the essence of the soundness argument for the full language. Concretely, FR remains close to Rusts surface syntax and captures many key aspects of Rust, including copy and move semantics, mutable and immutable borrows, and lexical lifetimes. Moreover, FR is compact and establishes soundness guarantees.

sections.title:
4.1 Syntax
FR’s syntax is shown entirely in Fig. 7. We highlight some important details in order of appearance.
sections.content: In Rust, use of 𝑤 is copy if its type implements the Copy trait, Move vs. Copy is Syntactic. and move otherwise. FR instead makes this distinction syntactically: 𝑤 denotes move, whereas copy 𝑤 denotes copy. This design keeps the operational semantics independent of the type system. The type system (defined in 4.2) then enforces that copies are only performed on copyable values: 1:10 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He copy 𝑤 is well typed only if 𝑤 has type 𝑇 satisfying copy(𝑇 ), FRs analog of the Copy trait. In our mechanization, the predicate holds for 𝜀, int, and shared borrows, but not for mutable borrows or boxes. Treating unit as copyable is small adaptation relative to Pearce [41]. Lifetimes are Lexical. block {𝑙 𝑡 } is sequence of terms separated by semicolons and annotated by lifetime. Lifetimes 𝑙, 𝑚 annotate every lexical block {𝑙 𝑡 }. Lifetimes form partial order 𝑙 𝑚, which means that 𝑚 is inside 𝑙. This holds exactly when 𝑚s block is lexically nested inside 𝑙s, e.g., {𝑙 {𝑚 𝑡 }}. We note that modern Rust uses non-lexical lifetimes (NLL), which let borrow end at its last use rather than at the enclosing blocks exit. FRs lexical model admits substantially simpler metatheory while capturing the essence of lifetimes and remaining sound. The blocks lifetime is written after the opening brace, {𝑙 𝑡 }, rather than after the closing brace as in Pearce [41] (i.e., {𝑡 }𝑙 ). The two notations denote the same abstract syntax, but placing the lifetime up front makes it visible that the label is committed at block-entry rather than at block-exit. This matters for several typing rules in 4.2, which type body terms against the blocks ambient lifetime: the label must be fixed before the first body term is checked. It also matters for our partial syntax in 5, where partial block whose tail is being emitted must already carry its lifetime. Reference Values Encode Drop Responsibility. Reference values come in two flavors: an owning reference ℓ and borrowed reference ℓ . Dropping an ℓ recursively drops the location it refers to, whereas dropping ℓ does not. Encoding this distinction at the value level keeps the operational semantics independent of the type system. By contrast, the distinction between shared and mutable references appears only in the type system, not at runtime. Both shared and mutable borrows therefore evaluate to ℓ values, since they are non-owning references. Borrow Types Name the Borrowed LVal. reference type is either shared borrow & 𝑤 or mutable borrow & mut 𝑤; we write & [mut] 𝑤 if either mode is allowed, where the target is single lval (e.g., & 𝑥). Naming the borrowed lval directly in the type allows validating borrow safety by tracking overlaps and conflicts between borrowed and assigned lvals. In contrast to Pearce [41], we adopt FRs follow-up work [40], restricting the 𝑤 to single lval instead of set of lvals to establish cycle-freeness of borrows. This formulation is sufficient in the absence of conditionals. Partial Types and Values Track Moved-Out Storage. At runtime, partial value 𝑣 extends 𝑣 with the undefined marker , which marks slot whose contents have been moved out. At the type level, partial type 𝑇 extends 𝑇 with 𝑇 (a box whose contents are themselves partial) and 𝑇 (a slot moved out but with its former shape remembered, e.g., & 𝑥, so re-assignment can be shape-checked). Every value is trivially partial value, and every type partial type. The converses fail because only the partial forms admit moved-out markers. Source-Level Fragment. The reference values ℓ and ℓ arise only at runtime, as the results of evaluating box and borrow expressions; programmer never writes them directly. We call term source-level when no reference value appears as subterm. The language does not require distinguished surrounding block: top-level program is any term typable from the empty environments at the root lifetime 𝑙root. Blocks remain the construct that introduces nested lexical lifetimes.

4.2 Typing
Fig. 8 presents the main typing rules of FR that we use in later sections. Next, we start by introducing
the typing contexts and judgments, followed by the auxiliary functions, and then explain the rules.
We close by stating FR’s type and borrow safety and summarizing our Lean mechanization.
sections.content: Contexts and Judgments. Typing uses two contexts. The typing environment Γ maps each declared variable 𝑥 to slot type 𝑇 𝑚, recording its partial type 𝑇 and the lifetime 𝑚 in which 𝑥 Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:11 [T-Const] 𝜎 𝑣 : 𝑇 Γ 𝑣 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ [T-Box] Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇 𝑙 Γ1 box 𝑡 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ2 𝜎 Γ2 [T-Copy] [T-ImmBorrow] Γ 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑚 copy(𝑇 ) readProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) Γ copy 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ Γ 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑚 readProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) Γ & 𝑤 : & 𝑤𝑙 𝜎 Γ [T-Move] Γ1 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑚 Γ2 = move(Γ1, 𝑤) writeProhibited(Γ1, 𝑤) Γ1 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ [T-MutBorrow] Γ 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑚 mut(Γ, 𝑤) writeProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) Γ & mut 𝑤 : & mut 𝑤𝑙 𝜎 Γ Γ1 𝑡1 : 𝑇1𝑙 𝜎 Γ2 Γ𝑛 𝑡𝑛 : 𝑇𝑛𝑙 Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇𝑛𝑙 𝜎 Γ𝑛+1 𝜎 Γ𝑛+1 [T-Seq] Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ2 𝑥 dom(Γ2) Γ3 = Γ2 [𝑥 𝑇 𝑙 ] Γ1 let mut 𝑥 = 𝑡 : 𝜀𝑙 𝜎 Γ [T-Block] [T-Assign] [T-Declare] 𝑙 imm 𝑚 Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇 𝑚 Γ2 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ2 Γ3 = drop(Γ2, 𝑚) 𝜎 Γ3 Γ1 {𝑚 𝑡 } : 𝑇 𝑙 Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇2𝑙 𝜎 Γ2 Γ2 𝑇1 𝑇2 Γ2 𝑤 : 𝑇1𝑚 Γ2 𝑇2 𝑚 Γ3 = write0 (Γ2, 𝑤,𝑇2) writeProhibited(Γ3, 𝑤) Γ1 𝑤 = 𝑡 : 𝜀𝑙 𝜎 Γ Fig. 8. FRs typing rules for terms [41]. Grey boxes highlight corrections made by our mechanization. was declared. The store typing 𝜎 assigns types to runtime locations. 𝜎 is needed only when typing intermediate execution states; source-level programs are typed under 𝜎 = . The typing rules in Fig. 8 use one main judgment: Γ1 𝑡 : 𝑇 𝑙 𝜎 Γ2. It asserts that, in lifetime 𝑙, the term 𝑡 has type 𝑇 , and that evaluating 𝑡 transforms the environment from Γ1 to Γ2. Threading the environment through the judgment in this input/output style makes the type system flow-sensitive. For example, after moving from an lval 𝑤, the corresponding slot in Γ2 is marked moved out, so subsequent use of 𝑤 is rejected. Declarations and assignments similarly update the environment in way that affects subsequent typing decisions. These effects cannot be captured by traditional flow-insensitive type system, in which each variable has single fixed type throughout its scope. Fig. 8 uses three auxiliary judgments only as premises of the main rules: LVal typing, type well-formedness, and shape compatibility. LVal typing, Γ 𝑤 : 𝑇 𝑚, determines the partial type of 𝑤 and the lifetime 𝑚 in which it can safely be used. Type well-formedness, Γ 𝑇 𝑙, holds when every borrow in 𝑇 targets storage that outlives 𝑙. Shape compatibility, Γ 𝑇1 𝑇2, holds when two partial types agree in structural shape modulo lifetimes and undefined components, while still comparing the types beneath any undefined components. Each of these three has routine rules, so we defer to [41] for their full definition. Auxiliary Functions. The typing rules also use FRs standard auxiliary functions [41]. Each is defined by straightforward case analysis over its input so we summarize their roles rather than reproduce the full definitions. Several auxiliary predicates characterize accessibility. copy(𝑇 ) holds when 𝑇 admits copy semantics. readProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) holds when 𝑤 is currently mutably borrowed in Γ, whereas writeProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) holds when it is borrowed in either mode. mut(Γ, 𝑤) holds when 𝑤 is mutably accessible: no dereference in 𝑤 steps through an immutable borrow. 1: Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He Three update functions modify the environment. move(Γ, 𝑤) marks the value at 𝑤 as moved out by replacing the corresponding component of its slot type with 𝑇 , where 𝑇 is the components former type. write0(Γ, 𝑤,𝑇 ) updates Γ to reflect assigning value of type 𝑇 through 𝑤. drop(Γ, 𝑚) removes all bindings declared in lifetime 𝑚. Typing Rules for Terms. Each rule in Fig. 8 types one corresponding term form from Fig. 7. T-Const checks literals and runtime values against 𝜎 and leaves the environment unchanged. T-Box assigns box 𝑡 the type 𝑇 when 𝑡 has type 𝑇 , while preserving any environment changes caused by evaluating 𝑡. T-Copy and T-Move encode Rusts familiar distinction between copy and move semantics. T-Copy permits copying an lval only when its type satisfies copy and readProhibited is false. T-Move returns the lvals type, requires writeProhibited to be false, and marks the corresponding component of its slot as moved out via move. T-ImmBorrow and T-MutBorrow simulate Rusts distinction between shared and mutable borrows, establishing their mutual exclusion. T-ImmBorrow creates shared borrow only when 𝑤 is not currently mutably borrows, i.e., readProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) is false. This permits multiple shared borrows while excluding simultaneous mutable borrows. T-MutBorrow creates mutable borrow only when 𝑤 is not currently borrowed in any way, i.e., writeProhibited(Γ, 𝑤) is false. This reflects Rusts requirement that mutable access excludes any simultaneous borrows. Additionally, 𝑤 must be mutable. The borrow expression itself leaves the environment unchanged. Its borrow information is recorded only when the resulting reference is stored through declaration or assignment. T-Seq threads the environment through sequence of terms from left to right and gives the sequence the type of its last term. T-Block types {𝑚 𝑡 } in its own lifetime 𝑚. Its result type must be well formed in the enclosing lifetime 𝑙, which prevents borrows of block-local storage from escaping. After exiting the block, drop removes any bindings declared in 𝑚. The premise 𝑙 imm 𝑚, requiring 𝑚 to be the immediate child of 𝑙, corrects missing assumption in FRs typing that 𝑚 is inside 𝑙. We specify this as an immediate-child relationship, as one can always choose an immediate child 𝑚 in place of any assigned 𝑚 inside 𝑙. T-Declare first types the initializer and then extends the resulting environment with fresh binding 𝑥 𝑇 𝑙 . The freshness check is made in the post-initializer environment Γ2, rather than in Γ1 by Pearce [41]: otherwise an initializer can redeclare 𝑥 and return it to the environment before the outer declaration installs its binding. T-Assign types the right-hand side to obtain Γ2 and 𝑇2, then resolves the left-hand side in Γ2. The lval-typing environment Γ2 is another correction to FRs typing, since evaluating the right-hand side may re-type the path under 𝑤 (e.g., 𝑝 = {𝑝; 𝑚𝑢𝑡 𝑤 } moves 𝑝 out, thus making access to 𝑝 invalid). The rule then requires 𝑇2 to be shape-compatible with the destinations stored partial type and well formed for the destinations declaration lifetime 𝑚, updates the environment via write0, and requires writeProhibited(Γ3, 𝑤) to be false. Type and Borrow Safety. Pearce [41] establish type and borrow safety for FR: well-typed program does not get stuck, and if it terminates, it does so in store that respects the borrow invariant, i.e., free of dangling or duplicated owning references and with at most one mutable borrow per location. We leverage this result to treat FR as meaningful proxy for usefully complex and expressive type system, motivating our choice of FR. Mechanization and Corrections. We fully mechanize FR in Lean, including syntax, typing, operational semantics, and type and borrow safety proofs. In this process, we corrected the stated typing rules in several places. The main corrections to T-Block, T-Declare, and T-Assign are marked with grey boxes in Fig. 8. 5.1 Partial Syntax and Realization for FR
We first define FR’s partial syntax, which lays the basis for the sealor definition. It describes prefixes
of syntactically valid FR programs. We then define realization, which relates a partial-syntax string
to a full-syntax string obtained by extending the partial string. This relation is useful because
completeness and soundness connect partial programs with their possible full programs.
sections.content: Partial Syntax. We define partial syntax relative to FRs full syntax in Fig. 7, focusing on sourcelevel terms that may arise during autoregressive generation. Fig. 9 gives the resulting grammar of partial terms (cid:98)𝑡, which extends over full term by allowing one of its parts to be only partially generated. The first two cases, (cid:98) and 𝑡, respectively describe the two endpoints: no part of the current term has yet been generated, and generation has produced full term. Each remaining production follows one term form from Fig. 7, replacing the component currently being generated with its partial counterpart. For example, (cid:98)𝑣 denotes partial value, and copy (cid:98)𝑤 copy term with partial lval. {𝑙 𝑡; (cid:98)𝑡 represents an unclosed block with partial tail. The lifetime label 𝑙 is an internal annotation, fixed once the opening brace is generated, rather than text generated by the model. The production (cid:98)𝑤 covers both moves and assignments, which share prefix until an = is generated. Partial lvals (cid:98)𝑤 and partial values (cid:98)𝑣 complete the picture. They are not listed in Fig. 9 but are described in prose next. partial lval is either (cid:98), full 𝑤, partially generated variable name (cid:98)𝑥, or dereference (cid:98)𝑤 whose operand remains partial. partial value (cid:98)𝑣 is either full value 𝑣 or partially generated integer constant; the unit value 𝜀 is always full. For simplicity, we omit prefixes concerning only keywords, such as let mut, &mut, or prefixes of either. Such prefixes contain no partially generated term, lval, or value for the sealor to preserve, so we fold them into the case of (cid:98). 1:14 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He Realization of Partial Programs. It differs from sealing, which may discard or alter unfinished fragments. This distinction will be made concrete once we introduce SFR. sections.title:
5.2 SFR: A Syntax-Guided Sealor for FR
Fig. 11 defines our syntax-guided sealor SFR by case anal-
ysis over partial syntax. It maps each partial term form
to a full FR term. As required by the generic definition,
SFR is total. Prefixes outside the partial syntax are han-
dled separately by returning them unchanged, so that the
underlying compiler may report a syntax error.
sections.content: At high level, SFR abstracts type checking through syntax-guided transformation. Rather than deciding exactly whether partial term admits well-typed completion, it constructs term whose well-typedness is necessary for that of every possible completion, thereby preserving completeness. Accordingly, sealing preserves generated structure when it exposes useful typing obligations, while abstracting away other obligations when they require information not yet generated or analysis beyond syntax. Several cases therefore seal directly to 𝜀: copy (cid:98)𝑤, (cid:98)𝑤, & [mut] (cid:98)𝑤, and let mut (cid:98)𝑥. Their unfinished components must be resolved before the corresponding typing rules can establish useful premises. Here, 𝜀 provides well-typed placeholder with no additional typing obligations. Moreover, for let mut 𝑥 = (cid:98)𝑡 and 𝑤 = (cid:98)𝑡, SFR recurses only on (cid:98)𝑡. It drops the enclosing declaration or assignment, including the declared variable 𝑥 or assigned lval 𝑤, and hence their associated typing obligations. This abstraction is deliberately not maximally aggressive. With completeness preserved, we seek to retain as much soundness as possible. Fully generated terms are therefore unchanged, including both standalone 𝑡 and all already-full terms 𝑡 inside an unclosed block. Furthermore, SFR preserves partial subterms that can themselves contain generated structure relevant to later typing, rather than discarding them outright. Thus, it recurses on (cid:98)𝑡 in box (cid:98)𝑡, {𝑙 𝑡; (cid:98)𝑡, let mut 𝑥 = (cid:98)𝑡, and 𝑤 = (cid:98)𝑡. At the same time, we do not make SFR more sound at the cost of lightweight design. For instance, in let mut 𝑥 = (cid:98)𝑡 or 𝑤 = (cid:98)𝑡, one could use the type of 𝑥 or 𝑤 to further constrain (cid:98)𝑡. We do not pursue this in SFR, because it would require typing information beyond our syntax-guided design. This would add implementation cost without substantial benefit, since SFR is already sound on an important class of programs, as we show in 5.4. few remaining cases complete the definition. (cid:98) seals to 𝜀, since either no part of the current term has been generated or the generated prefix consists only of keywords. An unfinished partial value (cid:98)𝑣 also seals to 𝜀. Although partially generated integer already suggests type int, we choose this simpler syntax-guided rule rather than recover that information, at small cost to soundness. The bare (cid:98)𝑤 production likewise seals to 𝜀: before an = is generated, it remains ambiguous between move and the left-hand side of an assignment, and sealing it as either would prematurely commit Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:15 to one interpretation. Finally, for {𝑙 𝑡; (cid:98)𝑡, the sealor appends trailing 𝜀 and the closing brace after sealing (cid:98)𝑡, ensuring that the result is closed block.

sections.title:
5.3 Global Completeness of SFR
SFR’s design was motivated by global completeness, which we formally prove next. We first show
that whenever a partial term realizes a well-typed full term, sealing the partial term with SFR also
yields a well-typed term.

sections.title:
6 From FR to Real Rust
We now carry the methodology of §5 from FR to real Rust by presenting our Rust sealor, SRS.
sections.content: Inherited from FR. We design SRS following the same principles as FRs sealor SFR (5.2). It remains lightweight and syntax-guided in most cases, discards typing obligations when needed for completeness, and adds targeted analysis where doing so improves soundness. It preserves already-generated structure verbatim and recurses on the still-partial frontier. As with SFR, the goal is global completeness together with selective soundness. For each Rust feature, we describe its syntactic shape, the partial forms the sealor may encounter, and the sealor rules used to close those partial forms. For readability, we reuse FRs notation where applicable and introduce new notation only for constructs specific to Rust. Not Inherited from FR. Two aspects of this section depart from 5. First, Rust is much larger than FR. The Rust sealor must therefore handle constructs with no FR counterpart, as well as constructs that resemble FR but differ in syntactic structure or typing behavior. We discuss representative selection of such features across this section. Second, we give informal, per-feature arguments for typing behavior, completeness, and soundness, rather than the formal, mechanized treatment we gave for the whole FR. This is because our goal for this section is to directly capture the behaviors of rustc, the Rust compiler. Using an existing Rust formalization as an intermediate target would make correctness depend on that formalizations faithfulness to rustc, rather than on rustc directly, which is especially undesirable as Rust continues to evolve. The global completeness argument follows the same structure as 5.3: as long as each sealor case is complete with respect to the corresponding rustc behavior, the entire sealor and the generative compiler are complete.

sections.title:
6.1 General Design Choices for Rust
We describe design choices recurring across Rust’s features, before per-feature treatment in §6.2.
sections.content: Sealors S𝑠 RS and S𝑒 RS. Since Rust distinguishes statements and expressions, we introduce two sealors: statement sealor S𝑠 RS that seals partial statement (possibly an expression statement) into list of statements, and an expression sealor S𝑒 RS that seals partial expression into an expression with an unconstrained type. When an expression is desired, and its type can be inferred from context, the expression sealor is used. In all other cases, we use the statement sealor. Placeholders holediv () and holeval (). FRs sealor closes every abstracted case with the single unit placeholder 𝜀, since FRs control-flow-free terms leave no other typing obligation to discharge. rustcs typing rules for branching and looping constructs additionally require reasoning about multiple control-flow paths, e.g., that both branches of an if agree, or that loop bodys effects are consistent across iterations. To seal such constructs conveniently, we introduce two placeholders with deliberately different typing behavior. The first placeholder, holediv (), is simply panic!() in our implementation. Statically, panic!() has type !, the never type, whose expressions can be coerced to any expected type. It diverges: executing it never returns normally. Because the continuation after divergent expression is unreachable, rustc does not require it to satisfy the usual borrow-checking obligations. This helps 1:18 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He preserve completeness; otherwise rustc might report borrow errors caused only by an intermediate partial state, such as variable being moved out before later generated code would reinitialize it. This makes holediv() useful for sealing control flow branches that should not produce value. The second, holeval (), instead behaves as if it produces well-typed value without diverging. We implement it as call to generic helper function: const fn holeval()<𝑇 >() 𝑇 { panic!() }. The function body diverges at run time, but this divergence is not exposed at the call site. The call expression holeval () has the declared return type 𝑇 , not the body type !. The type parameter 𝑇 is instead resolved by type inference from the surrounding context. Thus, holeval() can instantiate to any expected type in the surrounding code while keeping the borrow checker enabled. more subtle reason not to use holediv() as the expression placeholder is Rusts fallback for the never type. When type inference is underconstrained, ! may fall back to the unit type (), causing spurious type or trait mismatch errors. The type parameter 𝑇 in holeval() does not undergo this fallback, making it safe choice when sealed code must supply an expression value. Suppressing Future-Dependent Errors During Generation. Sealing Rust sometimes requires treating rustc errors more carefully than simple accept-or-reject verdict. This is not peculiarity of our sealor, but consequence of Rust features whose well-typedness can depend on code generated later. For example, partial trait implementation may not yet contain all required methods, call may refer to function item declared later in the file, and an expression may have an ambiguous type that is resolved only by subsequent use. Such errors are required to be suppressed for completeness. Otherwise the sealed program could be rejected even though the missing suffix would make the final program well-typed. To limit the impact on soundness, we suppress errors only in the scope where necessary. For instance, missing trait items is suppressed only if it occurs inside the sealing of partial trait implementations and type annotations needed errors only inside the sealing of partial function bodies. When we arrive at fully generated program after the EOS token, we clean out all suppressed errors, such that rustc could catch all errors. Projecting Error Messages on Partial Programs. Since rustc checks the sealed program SRS (𝑐) rather than the original partial program 𝑐, its diagnostics refer to SRS(𝑐). Feeding these diagnostics directly to the model can be confusing, because SRS(𝑐) may differ from 𝑐, as in Figs. 4 and 5. We therefore project diagnostics from SRS(𝑐) back to 𝑐. During sealing, we maintain positional map of code copied verbatim from 𝑐 into SRS (𝑐). We then re-render rustcs diagnostics using 𝑐 as the reference code, mapping the locations of diagnostic highlights from SRS (𝑐) back into 𝑐. Inserted code such as holeval() has no source in 𝑐; diagnostics arising only from such code are suppressed by the module described above.

This is not false rejection, since no well-typed completion can exist once too many arguments have already been committed. For soundness, we preserve rustcs checking of the callee 𝑒0 and the completed arguments 𝑒1, . . . , 𝑒𝑘 exactly as it would occur in the real program, so any type or borrow error among them is still caught. Field Accesses. field access has the form 𝑒.𝑓 , where 𝑒 is an expression of record (or reference-torecord) type and 𝑓 is an identifier naming one of its fields. That is, 𝑒field ::= 𝑒.𝑓 . Field accesses are lvals, so they participate in move, copy, borrow, and assignment on the same footing as variables. Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:21 The partial syntax rule is (cid:154)𝑒field ::= 𝑒.(cid:98)𝑓 . In other words, the receiver is completed but the field has been cut off mid-token. For 𝑒.(cid:98)𝑓 , we only copy the receiver. We take reference to avoid moving out of 𝑒. This is necessary because the completion may be method invocation (either on 𝑒 itself or on field) that takes self by reference. That is, S𝑠 RS (𝑒.(cid:98)𝑓 ) = &𝑒. For completeness, the receiver of any partial field access is strict sub-prefix of every completion, so the receiver is well-typed if the completion is well-typed. In terms of borrow checking, we need to suppress E0382 (borrow of moved value) because fields of the receiver (other than the one accessed) could be uninitialized. Suppressing E0382 means we do not check if the field (or the whole record) is initialized until later. For soundness, we preserve the type and borrow-checking of the receiver in all cases. We do not attempt to check whether truncated identifiers can be extended to real field of the receivers type, as this goes beyond our syntax-guided design. Instead, we wait until the field becomes full. Other Expressions and Top-Level Items. We briefly summarize the remaining Rust constructs. Aggregate literals, including tuples (𝑒), arrays [𝑒], and record expressions 𝑆 {𝑓 : 𝑒}, are sealed by recursing on the trailing partial element with S𝑒 RS and then closing the aggregate. For record expressions, we suppress E0063 (missing field initializers). This preserves completeness because already generated elements are checked in order, while missing fields may still be supplied later; soundness is preserved for the fully generated elements. Closure expressions are sealed by sealing the body as an expression, using holeval() to close value-producing positions. Closures with partial signatures are skipped until the signature is complete, which delays checking of parameter types. Other expression forms, including unary expressions, return, condition-local let, parenthesized expressions, try, ranges, indexing, and casts, are handled uniformly: the sealor preserves fully generated components and recurses into the partial one. Constructs without bespoke expression rule use the fallback sealor described above. Top-level items are handled similarly. Function bodies are sealed like closure bodies. Itemcontaining constructs, such as module definitions and impl blocks, preserve the enclosing definition and all fully generated sub-items, then recursively seal the partial item at the frontier. For constructs without bespoke rules, including type definitions, type aliases, constants, statics, extern blocks, global asm!, and macros, fully generated instances are preserved verbatim, while partial instances are discarded until enough syntax has been generated for more specific rule to apply. Example Revisited. Three rules seal the partial program in Fig. 4 into the full program in Fig. 5. First, the function body is an unclosed block ending in the partial statement let prio = tag.(cid:98)𝑓 , so RS((cid:98)𝑠); holeval() }. Second, like the FR sealor (5.2), RS ((cid:98)𝑏) = { 𝑠; S𝑠 the block rule appends holeval(): S𝑒 the let rule drops the left-hand side and recurses on the partial right-hand side: S𝑠 RS (let prio = tag.(cid:98)𝑓 ) = S𝑠 RS (tag.(cid:98)𝑓 ). Finally, the field-access rule closes the partial access as &tag.

sections.title:
7.1 Experimental Setup
Models and Agent Harness. We evaluate seven recent coding models. The three frontier black-
box models, Claude Opus 4.8 [3], GPT 5.3 Codex [37], and Gemini 3.5 Flash [13], come from major
model providers and are explicitly advertised for coding. We also include four strong open-weight
models, Kimi K2.7 Code [30], GLM 5.2 [60], and two models from the Qwen 3.5 family, a large
sections.content: 1:22 Niels Mündler-Sasahara, Hristo Venev, Dawn Song, Martin Vechev, and Jingxuan He 397B-parameter model and smaller 9B-parameter model [51]. This selection spans broad range of model categories and capabilities. We evaluate each model twice on every task, sampling with temperature 0.6 whenever the provider permits configuring it. We use an agent harness with fixed control flow, similar in style to Agentless [58], to isolate the effect of feedback and enable clean comparison. The model generates files or structured edits, while the harness deterministically writes them to disk, runs fixed compilation commands, and feeds any diagnostics back into the next prompt. Tasks and Benchmarks. We evaluate two repository-level tasks for which Rust compiler errors occur frequently. Both tasks require filling Rust skeleton that defines struct and function signatures. We report two end-to-end metrics: compiler error rate and functional correctness. Compiler error rate is the fraction of generated outputs for which the Rust compiler reports at least one error diagnostic. Functional correctness is the fraction of generations that pass the tasks unit tests. To better understand the results, we also measure runtime, the number of diagnostics in each compiler-feedback report, whether errors are detected during generation, and how far detected errors are from their original locations. We list prompts for both tasks in C. The first task, denoted Translation, is based on CRUST-Bench [20], benchmark for C-toRust translation. Translation is based on subset of 20 complex instances from CRUST-Bench. The model is given the implementation of library and is tasked to translate it into Rust. We ask the model to translate each file at time, such that it uses the specified interfaces. to Rust translation is an important topic, as Rust is considered more memory-safe and similarly performant alternative to C. At the same time, large translations are challenging: the model must translate data structures consistently and adapt them to Rust interfaces across files. The second task, denoted UpdatedAPI, evaluates whether models can adapt to changed Rust library APIs. Each instance asks the model to implement small command-line utility that requires using recently updated API function. This setting is relevant because LLMs always have fixed training knowledge cutoffs and may therefore generate code against deprecated APIs. The benchmark tests whether the model can use compiler feedback about the changed API to repair its generation. We provide details about benchmark construction in A. Comparison. We compare three approaches. The first, LLM, samples directly from the LLM without compiler feedback. The second, denoted PC for short, adds standard post-generation compiler feedback: after each generated solution, the compiler is run, and any diagnostics are fed back to the model. If compilation fails, the model may either edit its previous output or regenerate new solution from scratch. This process may repeat up to 𝑛 times. The third approach, denoted GC, further incorporates on-the-fly feedback enabled by generative compilation  (Fig. 6)  . This tests whether generative compilation can improve over post-generation feedback by detecting errors early. During generation, generative compilation may detect compiler errors on partial outputs and restart generation, up to 𝑘 times. If the model has not produced valid output after these 𝑘 restarts, we allow it to finish generating full output and then apply up to 𝑛 𝑘 iterations of post-generation compiler feedback. In our experiments, we set 𝑛 = 15 in UpdatedAPI and 𝑛 = 20 on Translation, and 𝑘 = 10 in both settings. We ablate over the budget choices in A.5. Note that we cannot experimentally compare against constrained decoding because, to our knowledge, no constrained decoding implementation exists for Rust. Although our approach can check partial programs, it is not suitable for constrained decoding. This is because our approach guarantees global completeness, whereas constrained decoding requires global soundness, as discussed at the end of 3.1. Building constrained decoder from scratch for real Rust would be highly complex and costly, as discussed in 2. This contrast highlights key benefit of generative compilation: it is lightweight to implement and largely reuses existing compiler infrastructure. Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code 1:23 Table 1. Comparison of LLM, PC, and GC on two repository-level coding tasks (Translation and UpdatedAPI). Here, LLM denotes generation without compiler feedback, PC adds standard post-generation feedback, and GC further incorporates on-the-fly feedback enabled by generative compilation. Overall, GC further reduces compiler errors while improving functional correctness. We mark the best result in boldface and underline results that are not significantly worse, determined by paired difference test at significance level 𝛼 = 5%.
